summary: DeVault argues that Signal's security claims are undermined by its dependency on Google Play Services, refusal to distribute through F-Droid, and centralized architecture that prevents federation. He contends that Signal's founder Moxie Marlinspike deliberately makes self-serving decisions that consolidate his control over the platform while using surface-level arguments to deflect criticism. The post emphasizes that truly secure systems should not require users to trust a single service provider, and that federation and peer-to-peer communication would distribute security risks across many operators. DeVault concludes that Signal's design choices prioritize Moxie's control over genuine user security, particularly for the most vulnerable users who need it most.
key_points: Google Play Services functions as a rootkit that enables silent background updates with arbitrary permissions, making any phone with it installed fundamentally insecure

Signal's refusal to distribute through F-Droid, which offers cryptographic signing and human-reviewed packages, undermines its security claims while its direct APK download lacks proper signature verification
Signal's centralized servers and trademark restrictions on forks create a lock-in effect that prevents any competing client from communicating with Signal users
Truly secure systems should not require trusting the service provider — federation would distribute risk across many operators in different jurisdictions
Moxie's arguments against federation and F-Droid distribution are deliberately constructed to appear reasonable on the surface while serving his own interests in maintaining control
money_quotes: Making promises about security without explaining the tradeoffs you made in order to appeal to the average user is unethical.
If you make broad and inaccurate statements about your communications product being "secure", then when the political prisoners who believed you are being tortured and hanged, it's on you.
Here's what Google Play Services actually is: a rootkit.

Try searching the Google Play Store for "flashlight" and look at the permissions of the top 5 apps that come up. All of them are harvesting and selling the personal information of their users to advertisers. Is this some kind of joke?
Truly secure systems don't require trust.
An open source software team reads this article as a list of things they can improve on and gets started. Moxie reads this and prepares for war.
No fork of Signal to date has ever gained any traction, and never will, because you can't talk to any Signal users with them.
What were you going to do with that 50 million dollars again?
key_insight: Signal's centralized architecture and hostility toward forks and federation reveal that its design priorities serve its maintainer's control over the platform rather than the security of its most vulnerable users.
